musicalLanguage.description: T-Bone Walker's 'Call It Stormy Monday (But Tuesday Is Just as Bad)' (1947) is the quintessential slow blues shuffle and one of the most influential blues recordings for guitarists — it introduced jazz-inflected 9th chords into blues vocabulary decades before that harmonic sophistication became standard. This arrangement, in G major at 174 and notated in 6/8 (spelling out the shuffle's triplet feel explicitly), is enormous: 393 measures, with 55 exact-repeat groups. One repeat pattern recurs six times at an almost perfectly regular 48-measure interval (8, 56, 104, 152, 200, 344) — a spacing that maps naturally onto a 12-bar blues chorus notated across four 6/8 measures per bar.
musicalLanguage.structure: A slow 12-bar blues chorus repeated many times across an unusually long, extended arrangement — the recurring pattern at measures 8, 56, 104, 152, 200, and 344 (at regular 48-measure intervals through measure 200, then returning once more at measure 344) marks out the chorus boundary directly in the note data. Chord-window analysis doesn't confirm the stated G major's plain triads, or the specific ninth-chord voicings this deterministic pass can't detect (its vocabulary stops at sevenths) — C-rooted chords (major and minor combined) are the single largest group, covering 19% of measures against 6% for G-rooted and 7% for D-rooted, blues' blurred major/minor third rather than one settled chord per degree. Energy builds gradually and peaks right at the very end: the five busiest measures (373-388, all 15-18 onsets) all fall in the last 20 measures of the 393-measure form, before the final measure (393) drops to just a single onset — a quiet close after the loudest stretch of the whole piece.
keyMoments: Measures 8, 56, 104, 152, 200, and 344 are exact repeats of each other, at regular 48-measure intervals through measure 200 and then once more at measure 344 — this marks out a recurring blues chorus that the piece cycles through many times, a clear example of chorus-length repetition (the chord windows immediately surrounding every one of these boundary measures read C major, not the stated G major).
Measures 373 through 388, the final 20 measures, contain every one of the piece's five busiest measures (15-18 onsets each) — the arrangement saves its highest energy for the very end.
Measure 393, the final measure, drops to just a single onset — after the loudest stretch in the whole piece, the ending is a quiet release, not a big final chord.
The right hand's highest note, A#6, arrives at measure 316 — inside the long final buildup, but before the true density peak at measures 373-388.
The left hand's lowest note, D1, doesn't appear until measure 325 — very late in the piece, suggesting the bass register deepens specifically during that final extended climax.
teachingGoals: Slow shuffle in 6/8: learn the triplet-based shuffle feel this notation spells out explicitly — practice subdividing each beat into three rather than swinging straight eighths.

Long-form structural awareness: with the same 12-bar chorus recurring every 48 measures across 393 measures, practice recognizing 'which chorus am I in' rather than treating the piece as one continuous block.
Delayed climax pacing: the busiest measures don't arrive until the final 20 of 393 — build real patience so the ending doesn't feel like just another chorus.

Blues harmonic color: T-Bone Walker's real recording is famous for layering ninth-chord voicings into blues — a texture this deterministic pass can't verify (its chord vocabulary stops at sevenths). What the chord windows DO confirm is that the harmony resists a single clean reading centered on the stated G: C-rooted chords cover more measures (19%) than G-rooted ones (6%). Listen for that unsettled, blues-third-blurred color rather than expecting plain triads.
styleTips: Commit fully to the 6/8 shuffle lilt — this is not a straight-eighths blues, and the notated 6/8 is telling you exactly how to subdivide the beat.
Pace the whole 393-measure arc with real patience — since the true climax doesn't arrive until measures 373-388, resist the urge to play every chorus at full intensity.

Lean into rich, unsettled chord color rather than plain triads — both the real T-Bone Walker recording's ninth-chord voicings and this transcription's own contested major/minor chord-window reads point the same direction: richness over plain triads.
Play the final measure genuinely quiet — after the loudest 20 measures in the piece, a single soft note is the intended release, not an anticlimax to fix.
